bio.biography:
Quick Facts

  • Specialty: 400-meter hurdles and 400-meter sprint

Biographical Information

Sydney McLaughlin is an American gold-medalist hurdler and sprinter. Born into a long time of track and field stars, McLaughlin took up running at an early age, following in the footsteps of her older brother and sister. She graduated from Union Catholic Regional High School in 2017, where she was the first two-time track and field Gatorade Player of the Year. Following her high school career, McLaughlin attended and ran Division 1 track and field at the University of Kentucky. 

In addition to being a track and field prodigy, McLaughlin is a published author, and recently published her first book, Far Beyond Gold: Running from Fear to Faith, which details her struggles with mental health and self-image in the athletic world. 

Olympic Highlights

At the Tokyo 2020 Olympic Games, McLaughlin secured two gold medals and dominated the 400m hurdles. Additionally, she played a pivotal role in the 4x400m relay, contributing to Team USA's triumph.

In the Paris 2024 Olympic Games, McLaughlin earned another two gold medals and broke the world record in the 400m hurdles with a time of 50.37 seconds.

World Championship Highlights

At the 2019 IAAF World Athletic Championships, McLaughlin earned a silver medal in the 400-m hurdles event. She went on to win gold in the 4x400m relay as well.At the 2022 World Athletics Championships in Eugene, Oregon, she set a clock record of 50.68 seconds in the 400m hurdles.

U.S. Championship Highlights

In the NCAA Division I Indoor Track and Field Championships of 2018, McLaughlin broke the world junior record in the 400m hurdles. In June of 2022 at the USATF Championships, she clocked a time of 51.41 seconds in the 400m hurdles. She broke her own record once again at the 2023 USATF New York GP with a time of 49.51 seconds. At the 2023 USATF Championships in Eugene, Oregon, McLaughlin completed the 400m hurdle at a time of 48.74.

Early Career

In 2014, as a high school freshman, McLaughlin set a national record and earned the title of the world's age-14 best. However, her age prevented her from qualifying for the 2014 IAAF World Junior Championships, but she set a world record in her age group in the 100-meter hurdles with a time of 13.34 seconds. She later pursued the age-15 world best clock speed in the 400m hurdles.

In 2016, McLaughlin competed at the New Balance National Outdoor High School Championships, breaking two records and winning the 400m hurdle event. At the USATF Junior Championships that same year, she took first place again.

During the 2016 U.S. Olympic Team Trials – Track and Field, McLaughlin set the world youth best and world junior record in the 400m hurdles. In the 2017 New Balance Indoor Grand Prix, she broke the indoor distance medley relay world record. At the 2017 Arcadia Invitational, McLaughlin broke the national high school record in the 300-meter hurdles.

During her time at the University of Kentucky, she set a new world junior record at the NCAA Division I Indoor Track and Field Championships in the 400 meters with a time of 50.36 seconds. In her debut at the SEC Championships, McLaughlin also broke the collegiate record in the 400-meter hurdles, clocking in at 52.75 seconds to win the event.

Olympic Experience
  • 3-time Olympian; 4-time Olympic medalist (4 gold)
    • Olympic Games Paris 2024, gold (4 x 400m Relay - Women, 400m Hurdles - Women)
    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (4 x 400m Relay - Women, 400m Hurdles - Women)
    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 17th (400m Hurdles - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – gold (4 x 400m Relay - Women, 400m Hurdles - Women)
  • Years of participation: 4 x 400m Relay - Women 2019, 2022; 400m Hurdles - Women 2019, 2022
  • Medals: 4 (3 gold, 1 silver)
    • Gold – 2022 (4 x 400m Relay - Women, 400m Hurdles - Women); 2019 (4 x 400m Relay - Women)
    • Silver – 2019 (400m Hurdles - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Her teammates call her Soupy
  • She speaks fluent French and some Chinese
  • She works at a nonprofit called the Farmlink Project which targets food waste and food insecurity
  • She is a crossword and Wordle enthusiast
Career Highlights
  • World Junior Championships, Quebec, 2022 (Team Free Routine - Women, Team Technical Routine - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Position: Goaltender
  • Wanted to play hockey after watching the Winter Olympic Games Vancouver 2010
  • 36 wins during the 2024-2025 season set the single-season record for a goaltender at Wisconsin
Biographical Information

Ava McNaughton began playing hockey at age 6 after watching the Vancouver Games, as McNaughton "loved how fast everything was and how exciting it was to watch." According to her mom, Ava would volunteer to play goaltender whenever possible in youth hockey. 

McNaughton attended an elite girls’ hockey program at Bishop Kearney, a private school in Rochester, New York, for high school before heading to the University of Wisconsin, where she won a national title in her sophomore year under head coach Mark Johnson, one of the heroes for Team USA 1980 "Miracle on Ice" squad. After winning a national title with Wisconsin, McNaughton was named to the U.S. women's hockey team for the 2025 IIHF World Championships and made her debut for Team USA in a group play win over Switzerland. Off the ice, she enjoys exploring different types of music, playing spike ball, and water sports like wakeboarding and wake surfing. 

World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – gold (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2025
  • Medals: 1 (1 gold)
    • Gold – 2025 (Team - Women)
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 gold)
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, gold (Team - Women)

Quick Facts
Married to Leonard Armato. The couple has a son
Career Highlights
  • Won Bronze at the 2004 Olympic Games in Athens with partner Elaine Youngs, who also went to UCLA
  • Finished fifth at the 1996 Olympic Games with Nancy Reno and fifth at the 2000 Olympic Games with Misty May-Treanor
  • Inducted into Volleyball Hall of Fame on Oct. 30, 2009
  • Won an NCAA Championship with UCLA in 1990, her only season with the Bruines
  • DID YOU KNOW
  • ---
  • McPeak is a color commentator for the Pac-12 Conference and the NCAA Beach Volleyball Championships
  • Played her last professional beach match in August of 2009 on the domestic AVP Tour with partner Kerri Walsh Jennings
  • McPeak became the first woman worldwide (2002) to surpass the $1 million career earnings and retired in May 2009 with over $1.5 million in earnings
  • She ranks third behind Misty May-Treanor and Kerri Walsh Jennings for most career earnings among women's beach volleyball athletes
  • McPeak and partner Elaine Young were the first U.S. women to medal in the beach discipline at the Olympics, 2004, (Walsh Jennings and May-Treanor took gold in the final, which was played after the bronze medal match)
  • Won 28 AVP and 19 FIVB tournaments
  • Won two Manhattan Beach Open titles with Cammy Ciarelli (1993) and Elaine Youngs (2004)
  • Internationally, McPeak ranked in the Top 10 seven times between 1995 and 2004
  • Voted the AVP Most Valuable Player in 1993
  • Ranked first in the AVP in 2001 and 2002
  • Made her pro beach debut at age 18 in 1987
  • Competed at Cal her freshman year and was voted the NCAA Freshman of the Year
Olympic Experience
  • 3-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Athens 2004, bronze (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Sydney 2000, 5th (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Atlanta 1996, 5th (Team - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2001 – 17th (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 1997, 1999, 2001
  • Medals: 1 (1 silver)
    • Silver – 1997 (Team - Women)

More
Was adopted at four days old into the McPherson family. Has been an USA National Team Member for 10 consecutive years. Did dance and played the violin in her teenage years. Enjoys being around family and friends on her off time. Is apart of the not for profit organization, A Safe Haven For Newborns. An organization that helps newborns from the dangers of abandonment and prevent the crisis that mothers-to-be can go through. Her favorite Bible verse that she lives by is: Proverbs 3:5&6 “Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge Him. He shall direct your path.”

Quick Facts
  • Married to Payton Melphy
  • Enjoys coaching
  • Specialist in the U.S. Army

  • Favorite movie is Never Back Down
  • Christmas is his favorite holiday
  • Captained the AIG Men's Collegiate All-American Sevens team to a bowl victory at RugbyTown Sevens
  • Played his first international tournament when he was selected for the 2018 USA Sevens tournament in Las Vegas.
